title: Variable Scope 🔭
description: Learn where variables live and die within your methods.

slides.title: The Fortress 🏰
slides.content:
A crucial rule about methods: they are like self-contained fortresses.

If you create a variable inside of a method, it is **locked entirely within that method**.

The outside program has absolutely no idea that variable exists!

slides.title: Scoping Error
slides.content: Run this code. It will crash! `secret` only exists inside the walls of the method.
slides.codeSnippet:
def set_password
  secret = "password123"
end

# Try to grab the variable from the outside
puts secret

quizData.question: Can code outside of a method access variables created completely inside the method?

options.text: No, variables created inside a method are locked entirely within its scope.
Only if you use the 'return' keyword on them.

quizData.explanation: This concept is called 'variable scope'. The scope of variables defined within a method does not leak out to the broader script.

slides.title: The One-Way Mirror 🪞
slides.content:
Not only can the outside program NOT look in, but the method *cannot look out* either!

A method cannot access standard variables created outside of it.

The only way to get outside variables inside a method is to explicitly push them through using **Arguments/Parameters**.

slides.title: Passing Scope
slides.content: Notice how we safely pass the outside variable `money` right through the front gates of the method using the parameter.
slides.codeSnippet:
money = 50

def check_wealth(cash)
  puts "You have #{cash} dollars!"
end

check_wealth(money)

quizData.question: What is the primary way to get outside data inside of a walled-off method?

options.text: Passing it through explicitly as an Argument into the method's Parameters.

quizData.explanation: Arguments are the gateway. When you feed outside data into a parameter, the method can safely use it inside its walls.
